metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the AC2 oracle to also seed ≥2 code-review rounds, select the older one, and assert its distinct content renders — or split AC2 into two sub-criteria (one per review type) each with its own oracle.
findings.finding: AC2's stated oracle only seeds and verifies plan-review history ('seeding ≥2 plan-review rounds, selecting the older round') but the criterion text it is meant to satisfy covers BOTH plan- and code-review history navigation, leaving code-review round selection unverified.
findings.suggested_fix: Revise AC2 oracle: 'a live end-to-end test seeding ≥2 plan-review rounds AND ≥2 code-review rounds, selecting the older round of each type, and asserting its distinct content renders.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either define the `show` subcommand's scope and AC in one of the two children (or a new child), or remove the `show` dispatch arm from a3d7 and document it as a future placeholder.
findings.finding: Child a3d7 registers the `audit` CLI intercept as dispatching both `serve` AND `show` subcommands, but neither child defines, scopes, or specifies AC for the `show` subcommand. It is referenced but owned by no sibling.
findings.suggested_fix: If `show` is intentionally deferred, replace the dispatch with a 'not yet implemented' SystemExit (matching the disabled-path pattern) and document it as a future story. If it is in scope, add its specification to the appropriate child.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback UI state for each panel region when its Epic 1 source fields are absent (e.g. a labelled 'Not yet available — requires Epic 1 story X' placeholder).
findings.finding: The plan references a fallback rule ('This epic MUST NOT proceed to render a region until its Epic 1 field source has landed') but never specifies the UI's fallback behavior when a sidecar exists but is the pre-enrichment v1 format — i.e., when the UI is installed but Epic 1 story A/B/C has not yet shipped. The rule only says *when* not to render; it does not say what to render instead (blank panel, disabled label, error state).
findings.location: Data contract section — paragraph: 'This epic MUST NOT proceed to render a region until its Epic 1 field source has landed'

findings.finding: The plan states 'this epic MUST NOT proceed to render a region until its Epic 1 field source has landed', but no fallback rendering behavior is specified for the intermediate state where a sidecar exists but is schema `plan_review_result_v1` (missing the v2 fields `block_threshold`, `blocking_enabled`, `evidence`, `scenarios`). An auditor who runs the UI before Epic 1's v2 enrichment is deployed will get either an error, empty panels, or silently missing data — and the plan provides no specified degradation protocol.
findings.location: Data contract section: 'This epic MUST NOT proceed to render a region until its Epic 1 field source has landed'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default port for `rebar audit serve` in the plan (e.g. `127.0.0.1:8742`) and specify whether `--port` / config-key override is in or out of scope for this epic.
findings.finding: The plan states the server is 'bound to loopback only' but never specifies a default port for `rebar audit serve`, nor whether the port is user-configurable. An implementing agent must pick an arbitrary port, risking collision with existing rebar/reviewbot ports or requiring a user-visible choice not described anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Approach rationale / Acceptance Criteria — 'rebar audit serve' subcommand
findings.suggested_fix: Add one line to the Approach rationale: 'Default port: 8742 (configurable via `--port` flag; `[ui] port` config key is in-scope for story X).' or explicitly mark port configurability as OUT of scope for v1 with a hard-coded default.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pass-to-field mapping table (or prose) to the Data contract section specifying which enriched fields feed each pass's UI panel (e.g. pass-1 → `finding/criteria/location/evidence/scenarios/impact/suggested_fix`; pass-2 → `verification`; pass-3 → `validity/priority/decision/reason/block_threshold/blocking_enabled`; pass-4 → `coaching[]`).
findings.finding: The plan says 'each review's four passes shown including pass-1 findings, pass-2 verification, pass-3 determination with thresholds, pass-4 coaching' but does not specify how the four-pass breakdown maps to the data-contract fields in the UI — i.e., which fields from the data contract table feed each pass's rendering section. An agent must independently decide the pass-to-field mapping and UI structure (tabs? sections? collapsible rows?), which is a real design choice, not an obvious inference.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'each review's four passes shown'; Data contract table
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the Data contract table with a 'UI pass' column, or add a short prose paragraph like: 'Pass-1 panel: per-finding `finding`, `criteria`, `location`, `evidence`, `scenarios`, `impact`, `suggested_fix`. Pass-2 panel: `verification`. Pass-3 panel: `validity`, `priority`, `decision`, `reason`, `block_threshold`, `blocking_enabled`. Pass-4 panel: `coaching[]` entries.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a Decomposition section (or story table) that names and scopes both child stories so AC1's 'Both child stories are closed' is verifiable against a defined set of deliverables.
findings.finding: AC1 ('Both child stories are closed') references 'child stories' that are never described, enumerated, or scoped anywhere in the plan body, making the criterion an orphan that measures undefined deliverables and giving the implementer no signal about what those stories contain.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Decomposition' subsection, e.g.: Story 1 — `rebar audit serve` subcommand, [ui] extra, lazy-import guard, loopback server wiring; Story 2 — per-ticket audit page template (ticket details + plan/completion/code panels, history navigation, four-pass drill-in, per-finding threshold meter, completion criteria[] rows).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or extend AC3) that verifies the server binds exclusively to loopback (127.0.0.1/::1) and refuses or is not configured to bind to external interfaces.
findings.finding: The Approach rationale and Compatibility section both describe loopback-only binding as a deliberate security property of the server, but no Acceptance Criterion verifies that the server binds exclusively to loopback (127.0.0.1 / ::1).
findings.location: Approach rationale ('bound to loopback only'); Acceptance Criteria (no corresponding AC)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'Oracle additionally asserts the listening socket is bound to 127.0.0.1 (or ::1), not 0.0.0.0.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the FastAPI+Jinja2 optional-extra architectural decision (including the rejected static-HTML alternative) in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc so future contributors have a durable, discoverable reference.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — a server-rendered FastAPI+Jinja2 optional web UI as a new 'thin, lazily-imported' extra pattern — but does not specify that this decision will be captured in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a story task (or note in the Scope section) to create a short ADR or CLAUDE.md entry covering: (1) the decision to use FastAPI+Jinja2 as the UI layer, (2) the lazy-import/optional-extra pattern as the established precedent for optional web surfaces, and (3) the rejection of static HTML export for v1 with the stated rationale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify the `nava-rebar[ui]` extra's dependency composition (at minimum: `fastapi`, `uvicorn[standard]`, `jinja2`), and clarify that 'reuse' means adopting the same lazy-import *pattern* as `reviewbot` rather than depending on that extra — `jinja2` is a net-new dep not in the existing reviewbot stack.
findings.finding: The plan says it 'REUSES the reviewbot extra's already-shipped web stack' for server-rendered FastAPI + Jinja2, but the `reviewbot` extra (["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "boto3"]) does NOT include `jinja2`. FastAPI's Jinja2Templates requires `jinja2` as a separate package. The plan introduces `nava-rebar[ui]` but never specifies its dependency composition, leaving a child-story implementer with three problematic paths: (a) omit Jinja2 — templates fail at import; (b) redeclare fastapi+uvicorn+jinja2 independently — 'reuse' is misleading; (c) express `[ui]` as depending on `reviewbot` — unnecessarily pulls in boto3 (CloudWatch metrics, unrelated to audit UI). This is a mechanism gap that the plan leaves unresolved.
findings.location: Approach rationale section + Compatibility & rollback section ('nava-rebar[ui]' extra)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach rationale or Scope section, add: 'The `nava-rebar[ui]` extra will declare `fastapi`, `uvicorn[standard]`, and `jinja2` directly (not the `reviewbot` extra as a meta-dep, which would pull in boto3). The "reuse" is of the lazy-import pattern and the FastAPI app structure, not of the extra itself.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the approach rationale a brief acknowledgment of the stdlib `http.server` pattern (as used in `editor_server.py`) and why FastAPI+Jinja2 is preferred for this multi-route, navigable UI (e.g., cleaner request routing across history, finding-detail, and pass drill-down pages; built-in template rendering vs. manual string assembly in BaseHTTPRequestHandler).
findings.finding: The plan's alternative-selection section only discusses 'static HTML export' as the rejected alternative, but the codebase has a structurally closer precedent for exactly this use case: `src/rebar/llm/workflow/editor_server.py` already uses stdlib `http.server.ThreadingHTTPServer` for a loopback-only, single-user, read-only local web server with zero new dependencies. The plan's positive rationale never acknowledges this in-repo alternative or explains why FastAPI+Jinja2 is preferred over it, leaving the selection reasoning incomplete.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the Approach rationale: 'The existing `editor_server.py` (workflow editor) uses stdlib `http.server` for a similar loopback profile; for the audit UI's multi-route navigation (ticket overview, per-round history selection, per-finding pass-1..4 detail) FastAPI's declarative routing + Jinja2 templating avoids the manual route dispatch and HTML-string assembly that BaseHTTPRequestHandler would require at this page count.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief rationale in 'Approach rationale' explaining why the new `[ui]` extra uses FastAPI + Jinja2 rather than the stdlib `http.server` pattern already used in `editor_server.py` for the same loopback/single-user/no-build-step use-case (e.g., async, routing complexity, template reuse, or Jinja2 safety — whichever applies).
findings.finding: The plan does not acknowledge the stdlib `http.server` loopback server already used for an identical use-case (`src/rebar/llm/workflow/editor_server.py`), and does not justify why FastAPI + Jinja2 is preferred over that proven in-repo precedent for a single-user, read-only, loopback, no-build-step audit view.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'Approach rationale' to acknowledge that `jinja2` is a NEW dependency (not part of the existing `reviewbot` extra) and explicitly name it in the `nava-rebar[ui]` extra's dependency list (e.g., `ui = ['fastapi', 'jinja2', 'uvicorn[standard]']` or similar).
findings.finding: The plan asserts it reuses 'the `reviewbot` extra's already-shipped web stack' for Jinja2 server-rendering, but Jinja2 is NOT in the current `reviewbot` extra (`['fastapi', 'uvicorn[standard]', 'boto3']`). FastAPI's `Jinja2Templates` requires `jinja2` as a separate dependency; it is absent from the codebase entirely. The plan does not name Jinja2 in the new `[ui]` extra's dependency list, leaving the capability assertion unresolved.
findings.location: Approach rationale section; Compatibility & rollback section ('web deps live in a new `nava-rebar[ui]` extra')
findings.suggested_fix: In the data-contract or Compatibility section, list the new `[ui]` extra's concrete deps: `ui = ["fastapi", "jinja2", "uvicorn[standard]"]`. Remove the 'REUSING the reviewbot extra's already-shipped web stack' framing since Jinja2 is net-new.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the `nava-rebar[ui]` extra's pip deps explicitly (at minimum `fastapi`, `uvicorn[standard]`, and `jinja2`), and confirm `jinja2` is listed — FastAPI does not bundle Jinja2 and the existing `reviewbot` extra omits it.
findings.finding: The plan asserts FastAPI+Jinja2 server-rendered templating but `jinja2` is not in the existing `reviewbot` extra (which the plan says it reuses), and the plan never names `jinja2` as a dep of the new `nava-rebar[ui]` extra. FastAPI does not bundle Jinja2 — `fastapi.templating.Jinja2Templates` raises ImportError at runtime when `jinja2` is absent even if `fastapi` is installed.
findings.location: Approach rationale section: 'Server-rendered FastAPI + Jinja2, REUSING the reviewbot extra's already-shipped web stack'; Compatibility & rollback: 'its web deps live in a new nava-rebar[ui] extra'
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's Scope or Compatibility section, specify: `ui = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "jinja2"]` (and note that `aiofiles` may also be needed if `StaticFiles` is used). Do not rely on `reviewbot` as a base — the `[ui]` extra is a distinct consumer context.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion that explicitly asserts and tests the server binds exclusively to loopback (127.0.0.1/::1) — e.g. 'a test confirms the server refuses or does not bind to non-loopback addresses' — so the loopback-only access class is enforced, not just declared in prose.
findings.finding: The loopback-only binding — the plan's entire security contract — is declared only in prose rationale and never enforced or tested in any Acceptance Criterion, leaving the access posture undeclared at the enforcement level.
findings.location: Approach rationale ('bound to loopback only') and Acceptance Criteria (no AC enforces or tests the bind address)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'The server bind address is hard-coded or defaulted to 127.0.0.1 (not 0.0.0.0); a test asserts the resolved host config equals the loopback address and that the uvicorn/server startup call passes host="127.0.0.1" explicitly.' This makes the access class declaration verifiable rather than aspirational.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the 'plan-review v2 `_slim`' and 'code-review v2 payload' wire shapes (schema version string, added fields, envelope) in the Data contract section so Epic 2's implementer has a complete interface spec to code against.
findings.finding: The plan's data-contract table requires the Finding-provenance region to receive `scenarios`, `evidence`, and `suggested_fix` from 'plan-review v2 _slim' (story A), but the current v1 `_slim` in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` explicitly excludes `scenarios` and `evidence` as 'runtime-only carriers'. The plan never defines what 'v2 _slim' means structurally — which fields it adds, how the schema version is stamped, or how Epic 2's UI detects that a v2 (vs v1) sidecar is present. An implementing agent for Epic 2 cannot know the wire shape it will read.
findings.location: Data contract table — 'Finding-provenance (per finding)' row / Required fields column
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-table or schema block under the Data contract section that names the v2 schema identifier (e.g. `plan_review_result_v2`), lists the exact fields added vs v1, and states how the UI detects the schema version (e.g. `payload['schema'] == 'plan_review_result_v2'`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Formally define the `criteria[]` element shape `{criterion: string, met: bool, citation: string|null, kind: 'codebase-verifiable'|'operator-attested'}` in the Data contract section, including field types and whether each is nullable.
findings.finding: The plan's data-contract table specifies the completion panel reads `criteria[]` of `{criterion, met, citation, kind}` from 'story C's `criteria[]`', but neither the plan nor any existing schema defines this `criteria[]` structure as a formal contract. The current `completion_verdict.schema.json` has no `criteria[]` property — only `findings[]` (failures-only). The plan relies on story C producing this shape, but never specifies it as a named interface within this epic's data contract beyond the inline table row.
findings.location: Data contract table — 'Completion panel (per criterion)' row / Required fields column
findings.suggested_fix: Add a schema block in the Data contract section (or reference the story C ticket's formal output schema by name) that fully specifies the `criteria[]` array element, including types for each field and the closed `kind` enum.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify which child (or a new third story) owns and delivers the 'story E read layer' (data access for ticket + sidecar records) and add it to the sibling roster with its own AC.
findings.finding: Both children reference 'the story-E read layer' as a hard runtime dependency — a3d7's index route and ff6f's /ticket/<id> route both consume it — but no story E exists in the sibling roster and neither child owns, defines, or delivers the read layer. This leaves the data-access foundation unowned, making both children non-functional without an out-of-roster deliverable.
findings.location: child a3d7-fadb-e7e9-4e2c (Scope: 'one index route using the read layer from story E') and child ff6f-b655-4058-4308 (Scope: 'add the /ticket/<id> route consuming the story-E read layer')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a third child story (story E / audit read layer) to the epic that owns: a read-only data-access module (e.g., src/rebar/audit/reader.py) with functions for fetching ticket metadata, plan-review sidecars, code-review sidecars, and completion verdict criteria[] from the Epic 1 enriched storage; define its interface contract so both a3d7 and ff6f can depend_on it; give it its own AC covering the data-shape contract and error handling for missing/partial records.

findings.finding: The named success criterion 'navigable across the retained plan- and code-review history' has no corresponding acceptance criterion or oracle test, so an executor could ship a single-round, non-navigable page and pass all ACs.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 1 vs Acceptance Criteria bullet 2
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a third AC under 'Acceptance Criteria': '[ ] With at least two retained plan-review sidecars for a ticket, the audit page renders round-navigation controls and the end-to-end test confirms that selecting a prior round loads that round's findings (verifiable by a field value present in round N-1 but absent in round N).'

Alternatively, extend AC 2's oracle description: '…asserts on the rendered response (including that navigating to a prior review round loads that round's distinct findings).'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
